questions.question: Explain the difference between %y and %Y in the date command. What will be the output of date +%y for the year 2024?
How would you display the current date in the format: 15-Jan-2024? Write the exact command.
keyPoints: Use date command with format string

questions.question: What is the purpose of the -d or --date option in the date command? Give an example.
keyPoints: -d option displays date for a specified string
Does not change system time, only displays calculated date

questions.question: Explain the Unix epoch and how the date command can be used to display epoch time and convert epoch to human-readable format.
keyPoints: Unix epoch is seconds since 1970-01-01 00:00:00 UTC
date -d @timestamp converts epoch to readable format
Example: date -d @1704067200 displays the corresponding date

questions.question: Write a shell script snippet that displays the current date and time in the format: [YYYY-MM-DD HH:MM:SS]
keyPoints: Use command substitution with backticks or $()

questions.question: What is UTC time and how can you display it using the date command?
keyPoints: UTC is Coordinated Universal Time, the primary time standard
It is not adjusted for daylight saving time
date -u or date --utc displays UTC time
Useful for logging and cross-timezone operations

questions.question: Explain with examples how %A, %a, %B, and %b differ in the date command output.

Write a comprehensive shell script that: (a) Displays the current timestamp in epoch format, (b) Converts this epoch to a readable date, (c) Displays the date in DD/MM/YYYY format, (d) Shows the day of the week. Include appropriate comments.
keyPoints: Store epoch in a variable using date +%s

questions.question: Explain all the format specifiers used in the date command with their meanings. Also, write a command that displays: 'Today is Monday, 15th January 2024 and the time is 14:30:00' assuming these are the current values.
keyPoints: Use %d with ordinal suffix for '15th' (requires additional handling)
Combine multiple specifiers in one command
